section.title: The Seven Days
section.sectionRationale: The creation prologue is the opening movement before the first toledot heading at Genesis 2:4, so 1:1 to 2:3 is one deliberate unit sealed by the seventh day rest.
section.thesis: Before the drama of a fallen world begins, God speaks a formless void into an ordered, good, and habitable home; this is act one of the whole story.
section.frameNote: Two panels. Days one to three form the realms, light, sky and sea, land. Days four to six fill them. The seventh day stands apart as the goal.

verses.text: In the {{beginning|beginning}} {{elohim|God}} {{bara|created}} the heavens and the earth.
Now the earth was formless and void, and darkness was over the surface of the deep. And the Spirit of God was {{hover|hovering}} over the surface of the waters.
And God said, “Let there be {{light|light}},” and there was light.
And God saw that the light was good, and He separated the light from the darkness.
God called the light “day,” and the darkness He called “night.” And there was evening, and there was morning, the first day.
And God said, “Let there be an expanse between the waters, to separate the waters from the waters.”
So God made the expanse and separated the waters beneath it from the waters above. And it was so.
God called the expanse “sky.” And there was evening, and there was morning, the second day.
And God said, “Let the waters under the sky be gathered into one place, so that the dry land may appear.” And it was so.
God called the dry land “earth,” and the gathering of waters He called “seas.” And God saw that it was good.
Then God said, “Let the earth bring forth vegetation: seed-bearing plants and fruit trees, each bearing fruit with seed according to its kind.” And it was so.
The earth produced vegetation: seed-bearing plants according to their kinds and trees bearing fruit with seed according to their kinds. And God saw that it was good.
And there was evening, and there was morning, the third day.
And God said, “Let there be lights in the expanse of the sky to distinguish between the day and the night, and let them be signs to mark the seasons and days and years.
And let them serve as lights in the expanse of the sky to shine upon the earth.” And it was so.
God made two great lights: the greater light to rule the day and the lesser light to rule the night. And He made the stars as well.
God set these lights in the expanse of the sky to shine upon the earth,
to preside over the day and the night, and to separate the light from the darkness. And God saw that it was good.
And there was evening, and there was morning, the fourth day.
And God said, “Let the waters teem with living creatures, and let birds fly above the earth in the open expanse of the sky.”
So God created the great sea creatures and every living thing that moves, with which the waters teemed according to their kinds, and every winged bird after its kind. And God saw that it was good.
Then God blessed them and said, “Be fruitful and multiply and fill the waters of the seas, and let birds multiply on the earth.”
And there was evening, and there was morning, the fifth day.
And God said, “Let the earth bring forth living creatures according to their kinds: livestock, land crawlers, and beasts of the earth according to their kinds.” And it was so.
God made the beasts of the earth according to their kinds, the livestock according to their kinds, and everything that crawls upon the earth according to its kind. And God saw that it was good.

Then God said, “Let Us make man in Our image, after Our likeness, to rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air, over the livestock, and over all the earth itself, and every creature that crawls upon it.”
So God created man in His own image; in the image of God He created him; male and female He created them.
God blessed them and said to them, “Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the earth and subdue it; rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air and every creature that crawls upon the earth.”
Then God said, “Behold, I have given you every seed-bearing plant on the face of all the earth, and every tree whose fruit contains seed. They will be yours for food.

And to every beast of the earth and every bird of the air and every creature that crawls upon the earth, everything that has the breath of life in it, I have given every green plant for food.” And it was so.
And God looked upon all that He had made, and indeed, it was very good. And there was evening, and there was morning, the sixth day.
Thus the heavens and the earth were completed in all their vast array.
And by the seventh day God had finished the work He had been doing; so on that day He {{rest|rested}} from all His work.
Then God blessed the seventh day and sanctified it, because on that day He rested from all the work of creation that He had accomplished.

beginning.gloss: The first, the head of a thing. The whole book unfolds what this one word opens: there was a start, and God stood before it.

elohim.gloss: A plural form joined to a singular verb. It signals majesty and fullness, and many hear a first quiet hint of the plurality later revealed as Father, Son, and Spirit.

bara.gloss: To create. In the Hebrew Bible this verb takes only God as its subject; whatever creating is happening here, it is a work no creature can do.

hover.gloss: To hover or flutter, the same verb used of an eagle over its young in Deuteronomy 32:11. The Spirit is attentively near the formless deep, poised to bring life.

light.gloss: Light, made on day one, before the sun on day four. The point is theological before astronomical: light comes from God. Paul later reads this as a picture of the new birth.

rest.gloss: To cease, to rest, not from exhaustion but the settled rest of a finished good work. It gives us Sabbath and points forward to the rest that remains for God's people.

basic.context: This is the very first page of the Bible, and it tells how God made everything. Long ago, the people around Israel believed the sun, the sea, and the animals were gods. Genesis says something different and bold: there is one God, and He made all of them.
basic.hermeneutics: The main thing to see is simple: God made everything, and it was good. People do ask good questions here, like how long the days were, and Christians who love the Bible answer that in different ways. That is okay, because it does not change the big point that God made it all.
basic.originalLanguages: A few Hebrew words carry a lot. 'God' here is Elohim, a full and majestic name. 'Created' is bara, a word only ever used for what God does. And the Spirit 'hovering' is like a bird gently watching over its nest, ready to bring life.
basic.typology: Some things here are signposts that point forward to Jesus. God's day of rest points to the rest Jesus gives. The place where God lives with people points to the new creation. Adam, the first man, points to Jesus, the true and better man.
basic.christ: The New Testament tells us Jesus was there in the beginning, and that everything was made through Him. When God said, let there be light, it is a picture of how Jesus shines light into dark hearts.
basic.god: We learn that God is very powerful; He speaks, and it happens. He is good, and He makes good things. He is orderly, personal, and generous, and He even rests, showing us that both work and rest are good.
basic.crossReferences: Other parts of the Bible pick up this story. John 1 says the world was made through Jesus, and Revelation shows God making a new heavens and a new earth at the end.
basic.oneStory: This is where the whole Bible story begins. God makes a good world. Soon people spoil it through sin, but God promises to make everything new again through Jesus. The good world we see here is what God is working to bring back.

context.literary: The prologue to Genesis and to the Torah, opening the first of the book's toledot units. The genre is exalted Hebrew narrative with poetic cadence, not myth and not a science manual.
context.historical: Traditionally Moses, writing to Israel newly out of Egypt among Egyptian and Mesopotamian creation myths. Read against that backdrop it is polemic: sun, moon, sea, and man are not gods but creatures of the one God.
context.canonical: The Bible's first page, establishing creation as the act on which fall, redemption, and new creation all depend.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal God as the sole sovereign Creator who orders chaos into a cosmos fit to be His dwelling. The text signals its own structure of forming, filling, and rest.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes God's acts. The prescriptive threads are the Sabbath pattern, later grounded in Exodus 20:11, and the mandate to image bearers.

debates.question: What are the days?

views.label: Twenty four hour
views.case: The plain sequence of evening and morning.

views.label: Framework
views.case: The forming and filling pattern is literary, not a timetable.

views.label: Day age or analogical
views.case: Yom carries a longer or analogical span.

debates.bearing: None of these touches the main claim, that God authored everything that is.

study.originalLanguages: The load bearing words move as a set: bara marks a divine only act, Elohim holds majesty, the Spirit rachaph hovers with life, and God names what He makes, which in the Ancient Near East is an act of dominion.

typology.fulfillment: A type of the greater rest that remains for God's people in Hebrews 4.

typology.type: Eden as the first dwelling of God with man
typology.fulfillment: Prefigures tabernacle, temple, and the new creation.

typology.type: Adam the image bearer given dominion
typology.fulfillment: A pattern of the true Man to come, Romans 5:14.

study.christ: John opens by echoing this text, in the beginning was the Word, all things made through Him, John 1:1-3. Paul calls the Son the image of the invisible God in whom all things were created, Colossians 1:15-16, and applies day one to conversion, the God who said let light shine out of darkness has shone in our hearts, 2 Corinthians 4:6.
study.god: Sovereign and powerful, creating by word alone. Good, and calling His work good. Orderly, personal, and generous. Transcendent over creation yet immanent within it. A God who works and then rests, dignifying both.

crossReferences.note: Deliberately reruns Genesis 1:1 with the Word as agent of creation.
Paul quotes day one to describe the new birth.
The Sabbath command is grounded in the seventh day.
The motifs of light, image, and rest reach their resolution in the new creation.

study.oneStory: This is creation, the first act. The good world fractures in chapter three, but the same Creator calls Israel, comes as Christ, and makes a new heavens and new earth where the seventh day rest becomes unending. The very good about to be lost is what the whole Bible strains to see restored.

sermon.why: No link verified in this session. Candidates worth verifying for this passage include Edmund Clowney, D.A. Carson, or Sinclair Ferguson on Genesis 1.

translationNotes.note: Some translations render an initial dependent clause, when God began to create, rather than in the beginning God created. The difference bears on whether verse 1 is the first act or a summary title.

quiz.q: According to Genesis 1, how did God bring creation into being?

options.text: He spoke, and it came to be; He created by His word.
options.note: The refrain 'And God said' drives the chapter. God creates by His word alone.

options.text: He fashioned light, land, and creatures across six days.
options.note: True as a description, but it misses the heart of the account, creation by the spoken word.

options.text: He shaped and arranged matter that already existed.
options.note: There is a formless deep in verse 2, but the text presents God as originating, not merely rearranging eternal matter.

options.text: He defeated rival gods and the forces of chaos in battle.
options.note: Unlike the surrounding myths, there is no battle here; God simply speaks and it is so.

quiz.q: Light is made on day one, but the sun not until day four. Why does this matter?

options.text: Light comes from God Himself; the point is theological before it is astronomical.
options.note: Day one teaches that light, and life, have their source in God, not in the sun. Paul reads this as a picture of the new birth in 2 Corinthians 4:6.

options.text: It shows the days are ordered by God, not governed by the sun.
options.note: A true observation, though it stops short of the deeper point that God is Himself the source of light.

options.text: It is a small detail with no real significance.
options.note: The ordering is deliberate and load bearing, not incidental.

options.text: It proves the account is simply mistaken about science.
options.note: The text makes a theological claim about the source of light, not a modern scientific timetable.

quiz.q: What does the seventh day, when God rested, signify?

options.text: A settled rest from a finished, good work, pointing forward to the rest that remains for God's people.
options.note: The verb shabath means to cease, not to recover. Hebrews 4 takes this rest as a pattern of the greater rest in Christ.

options.text: That creation was complete and God stopped working.
options.note: True, but it misses that this rest points forward and grounds the Sabbath.

options.text: That God needed to recover from the effort of creating.
options.note: This misreads the rest as fatigue. God does not grow weary.

options.text: It carries no ongoing importance today.
options.note: The seventh day grounds the Sabbath command in Exodus 20:11 and points to Christ.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament connect Genesis 1 to Jesus?

options.text: All things were made through Him, the Word and the image of God; John 1 and Colossians 1.
options.note: John 1:1-3 reruns Genesis 1:1 with the Son as the agent of creation; Colossians 1:15-16 says all things were created in Him.

options.text: He was present at creation in a general way.
options.note: True but vague. Scripture says more: creation was made through Him.

options.text: He only enters the story later, at Bethlehem.
options.note: This misses His eternal existence and His role as the agent of creation.

options.text: Genesis 1 has no real connection to Christ.
options.note: The New Testament deliberately ties creation to the Son.

quiz.q: What is the central claim of Genesis 1:1 to 2:3?

options.text: The one true God is the sovereign Creator of all things, ordering chaos into a good and habitable home.
options.note: This is the main point on which fall, redemption, and new creation all depend.

options.text: God made the world in an orderly, structured way.
options.note: True, but it describes the manner rather than the central claim about who God is.

options.text: The passage is mainly about the length of the creation days.
options.note: The days are debated, but that debate does not touch the main claim that God authored everything.

options.text: The world came about by chance.
options.note: This directly contradicts the text, where God is the deliberate Creator.

questions: God is the source of all things, the fountain of living waters. Where are you tempted to drink from cracked cisterns, the substitutes that never satisfy, instead of from Him?
The Spirit hovered over the formless deep, and God brought order by His word. What in your life feels formless right now, and what would it look like to submit it to His word this week?
God worked, then rested, and called it good. Does your rhythm of work and rest trust His finished work, or your own striving? Name one change that would honour His pattern.
You are made in God's image and given dominion. How does that dignity change the way you approach your ordinary work, whether behind a wheel, at a desk, or in a home?
On the first day God said, let there be light, before the sun existed, because light comes from Him. Where do you need to ask God to speak light into a dark place?
